content.text:
{Each of you should use whatever gift you have received to serve others.}{1 Peter 4:10}
 {Mathematical discoveries, small or great, are never born of spontaneous generation.}{— Henri Poincaré}

Some sets of vectors are "redundant"—one vector can be written 
in terms of the others. **Linear independence** captures the 
idea of vectors that each contribute something new.

content.title: Linear Independence
content.content:
A set of vectors $\{\vect{v}_1, \ldots, \vect{v}_p\}$ in a vector 
space $V$ is **linearly independent** 
if the only solution to
\[
c_1\vect{v}_1 + c_2\vect{v}_2 + \cdots + c_p\vect{v}_p = \vect{0}
\]
is the trivial solution $c_1 = c_2 = \cdots = c_p = 0$.

The set is **linearly dependent** if 
there exists a nontrivial solution (at least one $c_i \neq 0$).

content.title: Testing independence in $\R^3$
content.solution:
Form the matrix $[\vect{v}_1 \; \vect{v}_2 \; \vect{v}_3]$ and row reduce:
\[
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 4 & 7 \\ 2 & 5 & 8 \\ 3 & 6 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
\to
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\]
The system has a free variable, so there are nontrivial solutions. 
The vectors are **linearly dependent**.

From the RREF: $\vect{v}_3 = \vect{v}_1 - 2\vect{v}_2$ (or equivalently, 
$\vect{v}_1 - 2\vect{v}_2 + \vect{v}_3 = \vect{0}$).

content.title: Properties of Linear Independence
content.content:
1. A set containing the zero vector is linearly dependent.

2. A set of one nonzero vector is linearly independent.

3. A set of two vectors is dependent iff one is a scalar 
 multiple of the other.

4. If a set is linearly independent, so is every subset.

5. If a set is linearly dependent, so is every superset.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Independence and Number of Vectors
content.content:
If a vector space $V$ is spanned by $n$ vectors, then any set of 
more than $n$ vectors in $V$ must be linearly dependent.

In $\R^n$: any set of more than $n$ vectors is dependent.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Independence of polynomials
content.problem: Show that $\{1, x, x^2\}$ is linearly independent in $\mathcal{P}_2$.
content.solution:
Suppose $c_0 + c_1 x + c_2 x^2 = 0$ for all $x$. This polynomial is 
identically zero only if all coefficients are zero: $c_0 = c_1 = c_2 = 0$. 
Thus the set is linearly independent.

marginNotes.text:
There are different kinds of 
gifts, but the same Spirit distributes them.
